pairs.question: When was Camp Hearne commissioned and decommissioned?
pairs.answer: Camp Hearne was commissioned in 1942 and decommissioned in 1946.

pairs.question: How did the German POWs at Camp Hearne spend their spare time?
pairs.answer: In their spare time, the German POWs at Camp Hearne constructed detailed replicas of old German castles, including turrets and moats, some of which can still be examined today.

pairs.question: What was the role of Camp Hearne as a prisoner-of-war camp during WWII?
pairs.answer: Camp Hearne served as a prisoner-of-war camp housing prisoners from all three Axis powers and acted as the central prisoner-of-war postal service, responsible for distributing all mail to POWs in the United States.

pairs.question: Can you describe the incident involving Hugo Krauss at Camp Hearne?
pairs.answer: On December 17, 1943, Hugo Krauss was fatally beaten by a mob of six to 10 fellow German POWs. Before Camp Hearne closed in 1946, one of the attackers, Günther Meisel, confessed. Meisel and six others were convicted of premeditated murder and initially sentenced to life in prison. Their sentences were later reduced, and they were released in 1949 under an order by President Harry S. Truman.

pairs.question: What nickname did some residents give Camp Hearne and why?
pairs.answer: Some residents nicknamed Camp Hearne 'The Fritz Ritz' because they believed the German POWs were treated too well, comparing the camp to the luxury Fritz Hotel chain.

pairs.question: Which German writer centered a novel around Camp Hearne and its events?
pairs.answer: German writer Hannes Köhler centered his 2018 novel *Ein mögliches Leben* (One Possible Life) around Camp Hearne and the events among the German POWs stationed there.

pairs.question: What remains of Camp Hearne today?
pairs.answer: Today, a single replica of a barracks stands on the site of the former Camp Hearne, which contains a museum.
